metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'rollback test OR documented analysis' OR in AC4 by specifying the required artifact: prefer a concrete rollback test (e.g., instantiate the record file in a temp store, invoke the pre-v1.0 code path via a parameterised fixture or version-pinned subprocess, and assert the store is still operable); if a full mechanical test is impractical, state that explicitly with a rationale and require the compensating-forward path to be listed in `docs/migrations.md` with a linked Gerrit change — so the criterion is unambiguously evaluable.
findings.finding: AC4 offers 'a rollback test OR documented analysis' without specifying which is required or when analysis is acceptable instead of a test. This defers a real design choice to the executor, and if the executor selects 'documented analysis', there is no mechanically verifiable proving command — 'confirms a pre-v1.0 binary encountering the committed record leaves the store operable' cannot be demonstrated by prose alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'A rollback test (or documented analysis) confirms...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC4 with: 'A test in `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py` (or a dedicated `test_rollback_compat.py`) simulates a pre-v1.0 binary by invoking the store layer WITHOUT the compatibility-gate code path (e.g. via a version-pinned helper or by temporarily registering a no-op gate), writes the committed record, then re-opens with the real gate and asserts the store is operable. If a full mechanical test is impractical, `docs/migrations.md` must document the specific file-format property that guarantees ignored-unknown behaviour (e.g. the record file is in a subdirectory the old binary never reads) plus the compensating path, and a Gerrit change ID must be recorded on this ticket as attestation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving test and observable specification to AC5: name the test file (e.g. `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py`), invoke `fsck` and a read-only inspection command against a store with an incompatible record, assert exit-code 0 (or a defined non-blocking code), and assert the unsupported version/capability appears in structured output (e.g. `jq -e '.incompatibility_reason'` on a JSON report or a defined log field at WARNING level or above).
findings.finding: AC5 ('fsck, diagnostics, backup, and read-only inspection remain available under an unknown version/capability and clearly report the unsupported version/capability') names no test, no test file, and no proving command. 'Clearly report' is a subjective qualifier with no observable specification (no exit-code contract, no output format, no structured-output assertion).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC5 with: 'A test in `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py` invokes `fsck`, the backup command, and the read-only inspection path against a store whose record carries `format_version: 99` or `required_capabilities: ["unknown-v99"]`, asserts each exits with code 0 (or a defined read-allowed code), and asserts the structured output (or stderr at WARNING+) contains the unsupported version/capability identifier — verified with `jq -e` or a regex anchor against the exact field name.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` (the inbound event write path that directly acquires write_lock) as a consumer requiring the compatibility gate, or document explicitly why the reconciler subprocess is exempt and how the gate is enforced at a higher level before the subprocess is spawned.
findings.finding: The reconciler's inbound write path in src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py directly acquires write_lock and writes event files to the tracker store — this is a live store-mutation path that the plan does not enumerate as a consumer requiring the gate.
findings.location: Scope section ('wired into the write/compact/push/reconcile/MCP-write/repair entry points')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC #3's entry-point list to cover ALL mutating/publishing operations named in the 'What' and Scope sections (claim, delete, archive, MCP write tools, automatic repair, migration execution) so no described gate wiring is left without a covering test assertion.
findings.finding: The 'What' section specifies the compatibility gate must run before a broader set of entry points ("event append, transition, claim, compaction / snapshot rebuild, delete, archive, Jira push / reconcile, MCP write tools, automatic repair, and migration execution") and the Scope section reinforces "write/compact/push/reconcile/MCP-write/repair entry points", but AC #3 — the only criterion that verifies gate wiring — lists only "append / transition / compact / push / reconcile". The following described entry points have no covering acceptance criterion: claim, delete, archive, MCP write tools, automatic repair, and migration execution. An implementer who wires only the tested subset would pass AC #3 yet leave described entry points unguarded.
findings.location: AC #3 vs 'What' section (gate entry-point list) and Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC #3 to: '…invokes each mutating entry point (append / transition / claim / compact / delete / archive / push / reconcile / MCP write tools / automatic repair / migration execution), and asserts each raises the gate error / exits non-zero before any file is mutated.' Alternatively, add a separate AC that explicitly covers the remaining entry points if the list would make AC #3 unwieldy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC item 3 with the 'What' section: either enumerate all named entry points (claim, delete, archive, MCP write tools, automatic repair, migration execution) in the AC test assertion, or explicitly document which entry points are deferred and why.
findings.finding: The 'What' section enumerates 'claim, delete, archive, MCP write tools, automatic repair, and migration execution' as entry points that must run the compatibility gate, but AC item 3 only requires tests for 'append / transition / compact / push / reconcile'. An implementer can pass all ACs while leaving several named entry points unwired.
findings.location: AC item 3 vs. 'What' section paragraph 4
findings.suggested_fix: Expand AC item 3 to list every entry point named in the What section as a required test case, or add a sentence like: 'For this story, claim/delete/archive/MCP-write/auto-repair/migration-execution are wired but verified by inspection rather than dedicated AC tests; the following story will add per-entry-point AC coverage.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `src/rebar/_commands/fsck_recover.py` is gated (blocked when format is incompatible) or exempted alongside read-only fsck, and add it explicitly to the relevant list in the What section and AC.
findings.finding: src/rebar/_commands/fsck_recover.py is a destructive store-state modifier (drives git rebase --continue / cherry-pick in the tracker) that is neither named as a gated mutation entry point nor explicitly exempted alongside fsck/diagnostics.
findings.location: What section ('allow fsck, diagnostics, backup, and explicit read-only inspection') / AC line 5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'before any side effect' for Jira push/reconcile means before the Jira API call is initiated (required for full correctness) or only before a local git commit, and name the specific call-site in the reconciler/push code path where the gate check is inserted.
findings.finding: The plan states the gate should block 'Jira push / reconcile' 'before they acquire side effects,' and AC bullet 3 verifies gate blocking 'before any file is mutated (asserted by the store files being byte-unchanged).' But Jira push/reconcile side effects are primarily remote API calls (not local file mutations). The 'byte-unchanged' assertion correctly scopes to local files, but the plan's own description ('before they acquire side effects') implies the gate must fire before the network call, not merely before a local git commit. The plan does not specify at which call-site in the Jira push/reconcile code path the gate check is inserted — before or after the Jira API call is initiated.
findings.location: What section: 'Add a compatibility check that runs before any operation that mutates the store or publishes derived state: ... Jira push / reconcile'; AC bullet 3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exception class the gate raises on incompatibility (e.g. `StoreIncompatibleError` or reuse `CommandError`) in the plan's What or Scope section, and reference it explicitly in AC3.
findings.finding: AC3 says each mutating entry point 'raises the gate error / exits non-zero' but 'the gate error' is never defined as a named exception class in the plan. The new module is specified but its public exception type is not, leaving implementers to choose between CommandError, a new dedicated exception, or a plain ValueError — a structural gap in the gate's error protocol that affects every call-site that needs to catch or report the error.
findings.location: AC3 — 'asserts each raises the gate error / exits non-zero'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'The new module exports a `StoreIncompatibleError(Exception)` that the gate raises; entry-point callers convert it to a CommandError / non-zero exit as they do for other gate errors.' Update AC3 to name the exception.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) confirm via code inspection that pre-v1.0 binaries ignore the record file and state this explicitly in the plan, or (b) specify the concrete compensating-forward path (e.g., 'delete the record file to restore operability under old binary') so the rollback plan is explicit and actionable, not deferred to a future analysis.
findings.finding: The 'What' section states 'Confirm and document this [rollback safety] so rollback is safe (or document a compensating-forward path if it is not)' — meaning it is not yet confirmed that pre-v1.0 binaries treat the compatibility record as an ignored-unknown file rather than parse-and-error. AC #3 accepts 'rollback test (or documented analysis)' as the remedy. If the pre-v1.0 binary does parse and error on the new record file, there is no stated back-out plan — only a 'compensating-forward path' placeholder with no content. T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing, not merely an acknowledgement and a deferred analysis.
findings.location: 'What' section — Rollback safety paragraph; AC #4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' / Rollback safety paragraph: 'If pre-v1.0 binaries are found to parse-and-error on the record file, the compensating-forward path is: delete `_store/compat.json` (or equivalent) to restore the store to the implicit legacy state readable by old binaries. Document this step in `docs/migrations.md` and include it in the rollback runbook.' This makes the back-out plan explicit regardless of the analysis outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the gate's behaviour for a malformed/unreadable compatibility record (e.g. treat as fatal fail-closed with a clear error message, or treat as absent/legacy with a warning), and ensure the record is written atomically (write-to-temp + rename) to eliminate the partial-write case.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how a malformed or partially-written compatibility record file is treated. A corrupt record (e.g. from a partial write, disk full, or interrupted flush) is neither 'absent' nor 'present but incompatible' under the plan's two-case model, leaving the gate's behaviour undefined in this scenario.
findings.location: What section — absent-record vs present-but-incompatible policy; AC item 2 and 3
findings.suggested_fix: Write the record via a write-to-tempfile-then-atomic-rename pattern (standard for all store file mutations). Define policy: a record that exists but cannot be parsed is treated as fail-closed (not absent), with a clear error message directing the user to inspect or remove the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact post-rollback store schema state: assert whether the committed compat-record file (a) survives untouched on the tickets branch after a pre-v1.0 binary runs its ensure sweep and fsck, (b) cannot be accidentally added to .gitignore or deleted by any old-binary ensure unit, and (c) is found by a re-upgrading v1.0 binary in the expected state — attach a `DATA LOSS RISK` note if any scenario is ambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the concrete schema STATE of the committed compat-record file after a rollback to a pre-v1.0 binary — it only requires that the store remain 'operable' and that no 'parse-and-error or destructive compaction/fsck interaction' occur, but does not commit to what the file looks like post-rollback or how a subsequent re-upgrade sees it.
findings.location: AC item 3 ('A rollback test (or documented analysis) confirms…') and 'What' section bullet 'Rollback safety'
findings.suggested_fix: In the rollback-safety section, add a concrete state table: 'After rollback, the compat-record file (e.g. `.store-compat.json`) remains committed on the tickets branch unchanged because: (1) old-binary ensure units only touch .gitignore/.gitattributes/.env-id/gc-config and are tree-check-before-commit idempotent, so they will not stage or delete the compat file; (2) the compat file is NOT listed in .gitignore (and must not be — it is a committed record, not a runtime artifact); (3) compact.py only processes files inside per-ticket subdirectories, so the store-root compat file is unreachable by compact. On re-upgrade, v1.0 finds the record present and reads the stored format_version.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC or What-section clause specifying the gate's behaviour when the compatibility record is present but unreadable/corrupt (e.g. 'treat as incompatible / fail-closed with a diagnostic message naming the parse error', or 'treat as absent / pass-through and log a warning'), so implementers are not left to guess.
findings.finding: The plan specifies treatment for an 'absent' record (pass-through) and a 'present but incompatible' record (blocked), but provides no fallback for a third state: a record file that is present but unreadable or corrupt (JSON parse error, permission denied, truncated file). The gate's behaviour in this case is unspecified.
findings.location: What section — 'Absent-record (pre-v1.0 store) policy'; AC2 and AC3

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the initial recognised-capability vocabulary (even if empty `frozenset()`) and specify where it lives (e.g. a `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES` constant in the new compatibility module), co-located with the gate decision rule.
findings.finding: The `required_capabilities` array is a referenced schema field whose value vocabulary (the set of valid/recognised capability strings this binary understands) is never defined anywhere in the plan. The gate must compare entries against a 'recognised' set, but no such registry, naming convention, or enumeration is specified — an executor cannot implement the comparison logic.
findings.location: What section — 'required_capabilities'; AC3 — 'unrecognised required capability'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the new module spec: 'KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str] = frozenset() — the set of capability strings this binary recognises; the gate blocks if any required_capabilities entry is not in this set. Future capabilities are added here when the feature ships.' State this in the What section or Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the write path (event_append / txn / compact) writes the compatibility record inline when absent (rather than relying solely on the fail-silent ensure sweep), or document why the ensure sweep's fail-silent/skip-on-contention behavior is acceptable for the upgrade-in-place guarantee.
findings.finding: The plan designates `run_ensures` (which is explicitly fail-silent: LockTimeout → silently skips the entire sweep, logged but not surfaced to the caller) as the mechanism to write the compatibility record on 'the next covered write / init / fsck-repair / MCP-boot.' This means the record may never be written if the lock is contended during the ensure sweep, yet the gate correctly falls back to absent=pass-through on read. The plan does not specify whether the write path itself (event_append, txn, compact) should write the record inline when it is absent, rather than relying solely on the ensure sweep. If the ensure sweep is the only writer, a store under sustained lock contention could remain without the record indefinitely, silently re-treating every write as a legacy store instead of completing the migration.
findings.location: What section: 'Absent-record (pre-v1.0 store) policy — expand phase'; AC bullet 2: 'ensure-registry idempotently writes the record on the next covered write / init / fsck-repair / MCP-boot'
findings.suggested_fix: The init path (which runs before any write under the init lock) is a reliable place to guarantee the record is written, since it already holds its own init lock and calls _run_ensures_logged. Consider making the record write a synchronous step in init_core's bootstrap phase (not only in the ensure sweep) so upgrade-in-place is guaranteed at first init contact regardless of lock contention in the ensure sweep.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify SCHEMA_VERSION's fate in the Scope/AC: either document it as a distinct non-compatibility stamp (and explain the distinction from format_version in docs/migrations.md), or retire/rename it as part of this change — prevent two version-like artifacts with undocumented relationship.
findings.finding: SCHEMA_VERSION in reducer/_version.py is described as 'a stamp, never compared to fail closed' but its fate after this change is unspecified — post-implementation there will be two version-like artifacts (SCHEMA_VERSION and format_version), one of which is non-functional for compatibility. This strands an existing reference and creates confusion for future maintainers.
findings.location: Why section ('SCHEMA_VERSION is a stamp, never compared to fail closed'); Scope ('src/rebar/reducer/_version.py')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'In _version.py, retain SCHEMA_VERSION as an event-schema stamp (distinct from store-format compatibility) and add a comment distinguishing it from format_version; update docs/migrations.md to define both terms and their respective roles.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/init.py` (`_GITIGNORE` constant) to Scope as an edit target to ensure the compatibility record filename is git-ignored, and verify/document that the record's filename convention is excluded from compact.py's event-candidate scan.
findings.finding: The compatibility record file will need its name added to .gitignore (the _GITIGNORE constant in src/rebar/_commands/init.py) for old binaries to treat it as an 'ignored-unknown' file during rollback — but init.py/_GITIGNORE is not named in Scope.
findings.location: What section ('Rollback safety… the record file must be an ignored-unknown file to old binaries') / Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Name the compatibility record with a leading dot (e.g., `.store-compat.json`) so compact.py's existing `not f.startswith('.')` filter excludes it automatically, and add it to _GITIGNORE.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to Scope (or What): specify that the compatibility gate is exposed as a single callable (e.g. `assert_compatible(store_path)` in the new `_store/compat.py` module) that all mutating entry points call, so the coupling is through one interface rather than per-site logic.
findings.finding: The gate's interface/boundary design is unspecified — the plan says the check is 'wired into' many entry points (append, transition, compact, push, reconcile, MCP write tools, repair, migration execution) but does not define whether this is a single callable/decorator/context manager or a per-site inline check, leaving the coupling pattern open to inconsistent implementation.
findings.location: What section ('Add a compatibility check that runs before any operation...'); Scope ('wired into the write/compact/push/reconcile/MCP-write/repair entry points')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for the new module, write: 'Expose a single `assert_store_compatible(store_path)` function; all mutating entry points call this one function rather than implementing inline checks.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: gate rejections (unknown format_version or unrecognised required_capability) must emit a structured log entry at ERROR level (including store path, detected version, required capability) so operators can monitor and alert on blocked stores.
findings.finding: The plan does not instrument gate-rejection events with a log entry, metric, or alert. Operators cannot distinguish 'gate fired and blocked N operations' from 'gate is absent and everything is silently passing through'.
findings.location: What section — compatibility check; AC item 4 ('report the unsupported version/capability')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that the compatibility gate check is performed inside the write lock (not before acquiring it) for every mutating entry point, so gate check and mutation are atomic with respect to concurrent record updates.
findings.finding: The plan does not explicitly state that the compatibility gate check is performed atomically under the same write lock that protects store mutations, creating a potential TOCTOU window: the gate reads the record as compatible, then the record is updated (e.g. by a concurrent ensure-registry run upgrading it to an incompatible version) before the mutation acquires the write lock.
findings.location: What section — 'Add a compatibility check that runs before any operation that mutates the store'; Scope — 'New module under src/rebar/_store/ for the compatibility record + gate; wired into the write/compact/push/reconcile/MCP-write/repair entry points'
findings.suggested_fix: In the new gate module, make the check a function that must be called after lock acquisition; document this contract in the module docstring, and enforce it via an assertion or a context-manager API that bundles lock acquisition + gate check + mutation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the gate's error-reporting contract for each surface: what the CLI emits (exit code + stderr message format) and what the MCP tool-error response looks like when the gate fires, and add a corresponding assertion in the gate tests.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that an incompatible gate error must 'report the unsupported version/capability' but does not define the error-reporting channel or format, and the system has at least two distinct surfaces (CLI exit-code+stderr vs. MCP JSON tool-error response). Without a specified error shape, the gate may report incompatibilities inconsistently or silently across entry points.
findings.location: 'What' section, fail-closed bullet; AC item 5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/txn.py` (`transition_core`, `claim_core`) to Scope as an explicit wiring point for the compatibility gate, or document that transition.py/claim.py gate before calling into txn and that txn itself is not a direct consumer.
findings.finding: src/rebar/_commands/txn.py (which implements transition_core and claim_core — the actual locked-write cores invoked by both transition.py and claim.py) is not named in Scope, yet the gate must intercept before these cores execute to satisfy the AC requirement of blocking 'before any file is mutated'.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 3 ('The gate blocks mutation… event append / transition / compact / push / reconcile')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the new-module approach (e.g., 'to avoid import cycles with event_append, to be importable from compact/txn/push without pulling in the full write path'), and confirm the module dependency graph is cycle-free given that event_append, compact, txn, and push all currently import from each other in various combinations.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for placing the gate in a new module under `src/rebar/_store/` rather than integrating it into the existing `event_append.stage_and_commit` / `write_and_push` entry points (the current single choke point for most write paths). The plan specifies wiring the gate into many individual entry points, but the existing codebase already has a natural single choke point (`event_append.write_and_push`, through which 'comment/tag/edit/link/set_*/sign and the composer create/edit/revert path funnel') that could serve as the primary gate location. The rationale for a separate module (rather than an extension of the existing write-path gate) is not stated.
findings.location: Scope section: 'New module under src/rebar/_store/ for the compatibility record + gate; wired into the write/compact/push/reconcile/MCP-write/repair entry points'

findings.finding: AC3 references 'Experiment 2A' as the proving demonstration ('demonstrated by Experiment 2A: an old-build reconcile/compact/write exits before producing a side effect against a store requiring a new capability') but 'Experiment 2A' is not defined anywhere in the plan. An executor cannot construct or run an unnamed, undescribed experiment, making this AC unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'demonstrated by Experiment 2A' with a self-contained description, e.g.: 'verified by `pytest tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py::test_gate_blocks_write_on_unknown_capability` — the test writes a store whose `compat.json` contains `required_capabilities: ["unknown-v99"]`, then calls the append/compact/push entry point and asserts it raises `StoreIncompatibleError` (or exits non-zero) before any file is mutated (verified by asserting the store file mtime is unchanged).'

findings.finding: AC line 3's verification mechanism ('demonstrated by Experiment 2A') references a named experiment that is never defined, described, or introduced anywhere else in the plan. The verify step of a criterion must reference an entity described in the plan body; as written, implementers have no basis for what 'Experiment 2A' is, what it must produce, or where it lives.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC line 3: 'demonstrated by Experiment 2A: an old-build reconcile/compact/write exits before producing a side effect against a store requiring a new capability'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'demonstrated by Experiment 2A' with a concrete test description, e.g.: 'verified by a pytest parametrized case in tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py that opens a store carrying a record with an unrecognised required_capability (injected directly), invokes each mutating entry point, and asserts each raises the gate error before any file or external state is modified.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
